data.title: Why You Might Be Interested In Mills Locação, Serviços e Logística S.A. (BVMF:MILS3) For Its Upcoming Dividend
data.description:
It looks like Mills Locação, Serviços e Logística S.A. (BVMF:MILS3) is about to go ex-dividend in the next four days. The ex-dividend date is usually set to be two business days before the record date, which is the cut-off date on which you must be present on the company's books as a shareholder in order to receive the dividend. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Meaning, you will need to purchase Mills Locação Serviços e Logística's shares before the 22nd of April to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 30th of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be R$0.6617384 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of R$0.46 to shareholders. Looking at the last 12 months of distributions, Mills Locação Serviços e Logística has a trailing yield of approximately 3.2% on its current stock price of R$14.28. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Mills Locação Serviços e Logística's dividend is reliable and sustainable. We need to see whether the dividend is covered by earnings and if it's growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Fortunately Mills Locação Serviços e Logística's payout ratio is modest, at just 35% of profit.

View our latest analysis for Mills Locação Serviços e Logística

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. It's encouraging to see Mills Locação Serviços e Logística has grown its earnings rapidly, up 28% a year for the past five years. Mills Locação Serviços e Logística is paying out less than half its earnings and cash flow, while simultaneously growing earnings per share at a rapid clip. This is a very favourable combination that can often lead to the dividend multiplying over the long term, if earnings grow and the company pays out a higher percentage of its earnings.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Mills Locação Serviços e Logística has delivered 58% dividend growth per year on average over the past five years. Both per-share earnings and dividends have both been growing rapidly in recent times, which is great to see.

To Sum It Up

Should investors buy Mills Locação Serviços e Logística for the upcoming dividend? When companies are growing rapidly and retaining a majority of the profits within the business, it's usually a sign that reinvesting earnings creates more value than paying dividends to shareholders. This is one of the most attractive investment combinations under this analysis, as it can create substantial value for investors over the long run. Overall, Mills Locação Serviços e Logística looks like a promising dividend stock in this analysis, and we think it would be worth investigating further.

While it's tempting to invest in Mills Locação Serviços e Logística for the dividends alone, you should always be mindful of the risks involved. Litigation settlement reshapes cash flow visibility for Dorchester Minerals

Dorchester Minerals (DMLP) has settled litigation over leasehold interests in Midland County, Texas, with the agreement expected to provide proceeds to the Operating Partnership and support distributable income for unitholders.

For you as an investor, this resolution removes a source of legal uncertainty around assets in a key oil producing region and offers clearer visibility into how the partnership’s royalty focused model may translate into future cash distributions.

The litigation resolution comes at a time when Dorchester Minerals’ share price has a 90 day share price return of 21.68% and year to date share price return of 21.42%. The 5 year total shareholder return of 239.85% points to a very large long term gain compared with a more modest 1 year total shareholder return of 7.36%, suggesting recent momentum has picked up after a slower period.

With Dorchester Minerals trading at an implied intrinsic discount of about 62% while recent returns have been strong, you have to ask: is this still a value opportunity, or is the market already pricing in future growth?

Preferred P/E of 24.7x: Is it justified?

At a last close of $28.23, Dorchester Minerals trades on a P/E of 24.7x, which screens as expensive compared with both peers and the wider US Oil and Gas industry.

P/E compares the current unit price to earnings per unit and is a quick way to see how much investors are paying for each dollar of profit. For a royalty focused partnership like Dorchester Minerals, a higher P/E can indicate that investors are comfortable paying up for the quality and consistency of earnings, especially when those earnings are described as high quality and have grown over the past five years.

The picture is mixed. On one hand, DMLP reports high quality earnings and profits that have grown over the past 5 years, supported by a Return on Equity of 18.8%. On the other hand, earnings fell over the last year with a 38.1% decline and profit margins moved from 57.7% to 37.6%, while the dividend yield of 9.89% is not well covered by earnings or free cash flow. That tension between solid longer term profit growth and weaker recent profitability is important context when thinking about paying an above average multiple.

Against peers, the premium is clear. DMLP's P/E of 24.7x is higher than the peer average of 21.2x and also higher than the US Oil and Gas industry average of 14.8x. This suggests the market is assigning a richer valuation than both direct comparables and the sector more broadly, without a clear fair ratio anchor to point to as a possible landing point if sentiment changes.

The model projects future cash flows from the royalty and net profits interests, then discounts them back to today using a required return, which translates those future distributions into a single present value number.

This approach can matter for a royalty partnership where long term cash generation can differ from short term earnings patterns, especially given the recent drop in reported earnings contrasted with 5 year profit growth and a high current yield. On a P/E of 7.9x at a last close of ¥1,275, Daicel screens as undervalued compared with both its peers and the broader JP Chemicals industry according to the Simply Wall St model.

The P/E ratio compares the company’s share price with its earnings per share, so a lower P/E can suggest the market is assigning a cheaper price to each unit of profit than it does for similar companies.

data.title: Aptitude Software Group's (LON:APTD) Conservative Accounting Might Explain Soft Earnings
data.description:
The market was pleased with the recent earnings report from Aptitude Software Group plc (LON:APTD), despite the profit numbers being soft. However, we think the company is showing some signs that things are more promising than they seem.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Zooming In On Aptitude Software Group's Earnings

One key financial ratio used to measure how well a company converts its profit to free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio. To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

Aptitude Software Group has an accrual ratio of -0.16 for the year to December 2025. That implies it has very good cash conversion, and that its earnings in the last year actually significantly understate its free cash flow. Indeed, in the last twelve months it reported free cash flow of UK£9.3m, well over the UK£4.02m it reported in profit. Aptitude Software Group's free cash flow improved over the last year, which is generally good to see. However, that's not all there is to consider. We can see that unusual items have impacted its statutory profit, and therefore the accrual ratio.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

How Do Unusual Items Influence Profit?

Aptitude Software Group's profit was reduced by unusual items worth UK£1.8m in the last twelve months, and this helped it produce high cash conversion, as reflected by its unusual items. This is what you'd expect to see where a company has a non-cash charge reducing paper profits. It's never great to see unusual items costing the company profits, but on the upside, things might improve sooner rather than later. We looked at thousands of listed companies and found that unusual items are very often one-off in nature. And, after all, that's exactly what the accounting terminology implies. Assuming those unusual expenses don't come up again, we'd therefore expect Aptitude Software Group to produce a higher profit next year, all else being equal.

Our Take On Aptitude Software Group's Profit Performance

In conclusion, both Aptitude Software Group's accrual ratio and its unusual items suggest that its statutory earnings are probably reasonably conservative. Based on these factors, we think Aptitude Software Group's earnings potential is at least as good as it seems, and maybe even better!
